unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit you will learn how to describe people and things. You will learn words for what a person is like on the inside — such as kind, honest and friendly — and words for what things are like on the outside — such as rough, tough and smooth. You will describe your favourite people, compare two things, and tell true stories about the past. By the end, you will be ready to enjoy this unit's story about Nadia, a new girl who learns that carrying kindness makes everyone feel welcome.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few describing words each day.
Work through the seven short lessons in the Grammar file, one or two a day.
Read the Story file, “The Girl Who Carried Kindness”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Children describing people with kind and precise language

The word kind belongs to our words for a kind person vocabulary.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use describing words (adjectives) to talk about people and things.
Describe your favourite person and say what makes them special.
Compare two people or things, using words like kinder and rougher than.
Talk about the past, using was, were and verbs like helped and went.
Use the word could to talk about what someone was able to do.
Ask Wh- questions (who, what, why, how) about people.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write a short description of a person, using at least three adjectives.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: My Cousin Ishmael
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: My cousin Ishmael is my favourite person. He is kind and honest. He is always busy, but he still has time to help me. When I feel worried, he stays calm and speaks in a friendly voice. Ishmael is popular at school because he cares about everyone.

readings.title: Two Roads
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: There are two roads near my home. The first road is smooth and easy to walk on. The second road is rough, with many bumps and stones. My father says his old boots are tough, so the rough road is not a problem for him. The two roads are not similar at all!

readings.title: Describing a Friend
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “My best friend is Selma. She is very kind and friendly. Yesterday, I was sad, but Selma was calm and she helped me. She is honest too — she always tells the truth. Selma is my favourite friend!”

readings.title: Who Is Kinder?
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “My brother is kind,” said Amal. “But my brother is kinder!” said Adam. “He is friendlier than anyone in our street, and he could carry ten bags of rice when he was only twelve!”

readings.title: The Girl Who Carried Kindness
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The school bell rang, and the playground was full of noise. Children laughed, ran, shouted and played football. But in one corner, Nadia sat alone. She held her lunchbox quietly.
She was new. Her family had just moved to the town, and nobody knew her yet.
That week, the teacher gave the class a writing task. “Describe your favourite person,” she said.
“Can it be someone in school?” asked Ishmael.
“Yes,” said the teacher. “Write with care. Be honest. Use adjectives.”
Nadia thought for a moment. She did not have a favourite person yet.
The next day, something happened. Nadia saw a girl pick up a fallen bag and give it back to its owner.
“She could have ignored it,” said another pupil.
“But she didn’t,” Nadia whispered. “She cared.”
The girl’s name was Hannah. She was one of the most popular pupils — clever, kind, and always busy helping others.
One afternoon, the class went out to the garden. A rough wind blew, and papers flew everywhere.
“Someone catch them!” shouted the teacher.
Most pupils ran after the papers, laughing. But Nadia saw one boy trip over a chair. He looked hurt.
Sophia did not laugh. She stopped and helped him sit up. “You’ll be okay,” she said in a calm, friendly voice.
Nadia watched. Hannah was strong and tough, but also gentle.
That night, Nadia wrote in her notebook:
“She was kind to the boy who fell. She was friendly to the girl who dropped her bag. She could have laughed, but instead she showed care.”
The next day, Nadia found the courage to speak to Hannah. “Hi,” she said shyly. “I saw you help others. That was nice.”
Sophia smiled. “Thank you. My mother says, ‘Kindness is free, so give extra when you can.’”
Nadia smiled too.
The teacher gave a new task: “Compare two people in the class. Who is more helpful? Who is rougher?”
Some pupils wrote: “Ishmael is rougher than Hannah.” Others wrote: “Hannah is always calm and helpful.”
At the end of the week, the class shared their favourite-person descriptions. Ishmael said, “My favourite person is my uncle. He is tough, but he helps everyone.”
Then Nadia stood up. “My favourite person is someone who carries kindness every day. She doesn’t show off. She just helps.”
“Who is it?” the teacher asked.
Nadia pointed to Hannah. “She gave back a bag. She helped a hurt boy. And she gave me a smile when I was new.”
Sophia looked surprised. “No one told me that,” she said.
The teacher smiled. “Sometimes we forget the extra good we do.”
Later that day, Hannah and Nadia sat together under the tree. “You know,” said Hannah, “you are kind too. You noticed what others didn’t.”
Nadia laughed. “I used to feel alone. Now I feel a little more alive.”
The teacher told the class, “Describe someone again — but this time, include yourself. You matter too.”
Nadia wrote:
“I am similar to Hannah. I want to care. I want to grow stronger, not careless. I want to give kindness every day. Because I can.”

grammar.title: The Past Simple Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to talk about things that already happened. It helps us tell stories and describe what people did — like Hannah, who helped the boy who fell.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most verbs just add -ed:  play → played,  help → helped,  walk → walked.
Some verbs are special (irregular) and change all over:  go → went,  have → had,  see → saw.
The verb to be changes too:  is / am → was,  are → were.
Examples: “She was kind to her classmate.”  ·  “They played outside yesterday.”  ·  “He went to the store.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He goed to the store.”  ✗
Fix: go is a special verb: “He went to the store.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: The past simple is a time machine. When you use it, you travel back to something that already happened.
grammar.practice: Write the past simple form of the verb in brackets: | Last week, I ______ (visit) my grandmother. | She ______ (finish) her homework early. | They ______ (play) football at the park. | He ______ (go) to the shop yesterday.

grammar.title: The Word ‘Could’
grammar.explanation: Could is a helper word. We use it to talk about things that were possible in the past, or to ask for something in a polite way.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Past ability:  “When I was five, I could swim.”
Something possible:  “They could be calm even on rough days.”
Polite request:  “Could you lend me your pencil, please?”
Always use the plain verb after could:  could play,  could be,  could help.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He could plays football.”  ✗
Fix: use the plain verb: “He could play football.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Could is like a time traveller — it talks about what was possible before, or what might happen.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank using could + the verb in brackets: | When I was little, I ______ (ride) my bike without help. | If we practise every day, we ______ (be) champions! | ______ you please help me with my project?

grammar.title: Wh- Questions
grammar.explanation: Wh- questions start with who, what, when, where, why or how. They help us ask for exactly the information we want. Good learners ask lots of questions!
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The order is:  Wh- word + verb + subject.
Who → asks about a person:  “Who is your best friend?”
What → asks about a thing:  “What makes someone honest?”
Why → asks for a reason:  “Why is he so friendly?”
How → asks about the way:  “How do you get to school?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Why he is friendly?”  ✗
Fix: put the verb before the subject: “Why is he friendly?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: The Wh- words are your question detectives: who, what, when, where, why, how.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct Wh- word: | ______ is your favourite colour? (What / Where) | ______ do you go to bed? (When / How) | ______ is your best friend? (Who / Why) | ______ do you like the story? (How / Why)

grammar.title: Comparing Two Things
grammar.explanation: A comparative compares two things. It lets us say one thing is bigger, kinder or rougher than another.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short words add -er:  tall → taller,  rough → rougher.
Words ending in -y change y to i, then add -er:  happy → happier,  funny → funnier.
Long words use more:  helpful → more helpful,  careful → more careful.
Then use than:  “My dog is bigger than my cat.”
A few words are special:  good → better,  bad → worse.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “My dog is more big than my cat.”  ✗
Fix: short words add -er: “My dog is bigger than my cat.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of -er as a little “comparison badge” you clip onto short adjectives. For long words, add more — like adding “extra” to your comparison.
grammar.practice: Write the comparative form of the word in brackets: | This puzzle is ______ (easy) than that one. | She is ______ (smart) than her brother. | This story is ______ (funny) than the last one.

grammar.title: Present Simple Statements
grammar.explanation: We use the present simple to describe people and things, and to talk about what is true now. It is perfect for describing a friend: “My friend is kind.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use is for one person or thing:  “My friend is kind and careful.”
Use are for more than one:  “Popular kids are not always kind.”
With he, she, it, action verbs add -s:  “She plays the piano.”  ·  “He helps everyone.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He play football.”  ✗
Fix: add -s for he/she/it: “He plays football.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: S for singular! For he, she or it, remember to add -s to the action verb.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the correct form of the verb in brackets: | My sister ______ (like) to read books. | The dogs ______ (be) very friendly. | My parents ______ (enjoy) cooking together. | He ______ (help) his friends every day.

grammar.title: Telling What Someone Said + Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
grammar.explanation: Reported speech is how we tell someone what another person said, without using their exact words. It is useful for telling stories: “She said her cousin was busy.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with a reporting word:  said, told, asked.
Change the verb back one step in time:  “I am happy” → “She said she was happy.”
Change the pronoun to match:  “My cousin is busy” → “She said her cousin was busy.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She said her cousin is busy.”  ✗
Fix: change the verb to the past: “She said her cousin was busy.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Say it like a rhyme: “Change the tense, change the pronoun!”
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank to finish the reported sentence: | Direct: “I love ice cream.”  →  Reported: He said he ______ ice cream. | Direct: “Where are you going?”  →  Reported: She asked where I ______ going. | Direct: “Don’t touch that!”  →  Reported: He told me ______ touch that. | Lesson 7:  Imperatives (Giving Instructions) | What it means and why it matters | An imperative is a command or instruction. Your teacher uses them all the time: “Describe your favourite classmate.” They tell someone exactly what to do. | How to form it | Start with the plain verb — no subject:  “Describe your best friend.” | Use clear action words:  List, Write, Draw, Explain. | To be polite, add please:  “Please describe your favourite classmate.” | To say “don’t”, use Do not / Don’t:  “Don’t forget to add adjectives.” | A common mistake | Mistake: “You describe your favourite classmate.”  ✗ | Fix: leave out the subject: “Describe your favourite classmate.”  ✓ | Memory tip | An imperative is like pointing at a friend and saying the action straight out: “Describe!” | Practice 7 | Fill in the blank with a verb from the box:  (Describe · List · Draw · Write) | ______ your favourite animal. | ______ three things you love about school. | ______ a picture of your family. | ______ words that describe a good friend.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe Your Favourite Person
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say three or four sentences. You can start like this:
“My favourite person is ______.”
“He / She is ______ and ______.” (use two adjectives)
“He / She helps me when ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use at least two adjectives? Did you say who the person is and why you like them? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe your favourite person and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Describe a Thing
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Pick up an object near you. Describe it in two or three sentences:
“This is my ______.”
“It is ______.” (for example: rough, smooth, tough, small)
“It is similar to a ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe a thing and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Guess Who
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Think of a person everyone knows, but keep it a secret.
Describe them with three adjectives: “This person is kind, busy and honest.”
The other person asks Wh- questions: “Is it a girl or a boy? Why do you like them?”
Keep going until they guess. Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise guess who and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — A Description of a Person
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four sentences about a person you like. Use at least three adjectives from this unit. You can begin:
My favourite person is ______.
He / She is ______, ______ and ______.
He / She is good at ______.
I like this person because ______.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about a description of a person.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Comparing Two People
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write two sentences that compare two people you know. Use comparatives (see Grammar Lesson 4):
“______ is kinder than ______.”
“______ is busier than ______.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about comparing two people.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Sort the Traits
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make two lists. In the first list, write describing words about how someone acts or feels (for example: kind, honest, calm). In the second list, write describing words about how a thing looks or feels (for example: rough, tough, smooth). Check your lists with your AI tutor.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about sort the traits.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Choose the Right Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the best describing word for each sentence.
The rock was hard and ______ to break. (tough / calm)
She always tells the truth; she is very ______. (honest / rough)
He has many things to do today; he is ______. (busy / alive)
My shirt looks almost the same as yours; they are ______. (similar / careless)
Answers: 1. tough  2. honest  3. busy  4. similar
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Opposites
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to its opposite.
careful  →  ______  (careless / kind)
rough  →  ______  (smooth / busy)
dead  →  ______  (alive / tough)
unkind  →  ______  (kind / rough)
Answers: 1. careless  2. smooth  3. alive  4. kind
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Past or Now?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill in the blank with is (now) or was (in the past).
Right now, my sister ______ busy with her homework.
Yesterday, Hannah ______ kind to the new boy.
Today the road ______ rough after the rain.
Last week, the teacher ______ happy with our writing.
Answers: 1. is  2. was  3. is  4. was
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Describe and Draw
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw a person from your family. Around your drawing, write four adjectives that describe them. Then read your words to your AI tutor or your teacher.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Describing People and Things learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1 — A Description of a Person and a recording of Speaking 1 — Describe Your Favourite Person. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'kind' mean?
quizzes.options: Always tells the truth. | Nice and helpful to other people. | The act of being kind and helpful. | To help or worry about someone because you want them to be well.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Nice and helpful to other people.
quizzes.explanation: The kind girl shared her lunch with a new pupil.

quizzes.question: What does 'friendly' mean?
quizzes.options: Doing many things and having little free time. | Happy to talk to people and play with them. | The act of being kind and helpful. | To help or worry about someone because you want them to be well.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Happy to talk to people and play with them.
quizzes.explanation: Our new neighbour is very friendly.

quizzes.question: What does 'honest' mean?
quizzes.options: Always tells the truth. | Doing many things and having little free time. | Quiet and gentle; not angry or excited. | To help or worry about someone because you want them to be well.
quizzes.explanation: Amal is honest, so she told the teacher what really happened.

quizzes.question: What does 'kindness' mean?
quizzes.options: Can do something. | Doing many things and having little free time. | Quiet and gentle; not angry or excited. | The act of being kind and helpful.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The act of being kind and helpful.
quizzes.explanation: She showed kindness by helping the boy who fell.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'My Cousin Ishmael': Ishmael is popular at school because he _____ about everyone.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Ishmael is popular at school because he cares about everyone.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'My Cousin Ishmael': When I feel worried, he stays calm and speaks in a _____ voice.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: When I feel worried, he stays calm and speaks in a friendly voice.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Describing a Friend': She is _____ too — she always tells the truth.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: She is honest too — she always tells the truth.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Describing a Friend': Yesterday, I was sad, but Selma was _____ and she helped me.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Yesterday, I was sad, but Selma was calm and she helped me.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Girl Who Carried Kindness': She could have laughed, but instead she showed _____.” The next day, Nadia found the courage to speak to Hannah.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: She could have laughed, but instead she showed care.” The next day, Nadia found the courage to speak to Hannah.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'My Cousin Ishmael': He is always _____, but he still has time to help me.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: He is always busy, but he still has time to help me.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read ‘My Cousin Ishmael’ with me and explain the describing words?”
“Can you play a listening game where you describe a person and I guess the adjectives?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise describing my favourite person?”
“Can you play ‘Guess Who’ with me?”
“Did I use my adjectives correctly?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my description and tell me what to fix?”
“Can you help me make my comparing sentences correct?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me describe a kind person?”
“What is the difference between kind and kindness?”
“Can you give me other words that mean friendly?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on these five words?”
“Can you help me use careless and careful in sentences?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between rough and tough?”
“Can you give me two things that are similar so I can compare them?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you ask me about my favourite things?”
“In the story, someone says ‘give extra when you can.’ What does that mean?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘she was kind’ and not ‘she is kind’ when we tell a story about yesterday?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise comparatives?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Nice and helpful to other people. The kind girl shared her lunch with a new pupil.
Happy to talk to people and play with them. Our new neighbour is very friendly.
Always tells the truth. Amal is honest, so she told the teacher what really happened.
The act of being kind and helpful. She showed kindness by helping the boy who fell.
To help or worry about someone because you want them to be well. I care about my little brother.
Doing many things and having little free time. The teacher was too busy to talk right now.
Quiet and gentle; not angry or excited. Hannah stayed calm and helped in a soft voice.
Can do something. I am able to tie my own shoes now.
Not careful, so you make mistakes. A careless move knocked the cup off the table.
Liked by many people. He is popular at school because he is kind to everyone.
